questions.text: Who was the first female ruler of the Delhi Sultanate?
questions.explanation: Razia Sultana (1236–1240 CE) was the first and only woman to sit on the throne of Delhi. She was appointed successor by her father Iltutmish. She ruled unveiled, held public court, and was deposed by the Chahalgani (Forty) Turkish nobles and executed in 1240.

questions.text: Alauddin Khalji's market control policy established how many regulated markets in Delhi?
questions.explanation: Alauddin Khalji established four regulated markets in Delhi: (1) grain market, (2) cloth market, (3) cattle and horses market, (4) general goods market. Strict price controls were enforced by spies (barids). This is considered one of the earliest examples of state-controlled pricing in Indian history.

questions.text: Muhammad bin Tughlaq's decision to shift the capital from Delhi was to which city?
questions.explanation: Muhammad bin Tughlaq ordered the transfer of his capital from Delhi to Devagiri (~1327 CE), renaming Devagiri as Daulatabad. His plan was geographically sound (more central location) but the forced march of thousands of people in summer resulted in enormous suffering and death. He moved it back later.

questions.text: The Qutub Minar in Delhi was completed by which sultan?
questions.explanation: Qutbuddin Aibak began the Qutub Minar; Iltutmish completed the main three storeys and is traditionally credited with completing the structure. However, the last two storeys (the marble-striped ones) were later added by Firuz Shah Tughlaq after lightning damage. Iltutmish's completion is the standard textbook answer.

questions.text: Which Delhi Sultan was called 'Lakh Baksh' (Giver of Lakhs)?
questions.explanation: Qutbuddin Aibak was called 'Lakh Baksh' for his exceptional generosity in charity. He died in 1210 CE while playing chaugan (polo) — falling from his horse. He is buried in Lahore (Pakistan), not Delhi.

questions.text: The 'Iqta system' was introduced in the Delhi Sultanate by which ruler?
questions.explanation: Iltutmish introduced the Iqta system — assigning revenue-collecting rights over land parcels (iqtas) to military and civil officers in lieu of salary. Officers (iqtadars) were rotated to prevent them from building a permanent power base. This system was a key administrative innovation of the Sultanate.

questions.text: Ibn Battuta visited India during the reign of which sultan and wrote about his experiences in 'Rihla'?
questions.explanation: Ibn Battuta (Moroccan traveller) visited Delhi from 1333–1342 CE during Muhammad bin Tughlaq's reign. His account in Rihla ('The Travels') is the most detailed description of 14th-century Delhi. He described the Sultan as brilliant but erratic and unpredictable — capable of extreme generosity and extreme cruelty.

questions.text: Who was the first Sultan of Delhi to receive formal investiture (khilafat) from the Caliph of Baghdad?
questions.explanation: Iltutmish received formal investiture (a manshur/sanad) from the Abbasid Caliph of Baghdad — which gave his rule Islamic legitimacy. This was important because Aibak had no such validation. Iltutmish used this to assert that the Delhi Sultanate was a legitimate Islamic state, not just a military occupation.

questions.text: Agra city was founded in 1504 CE by which Lodi Sultan?
questions.explanation: Sikandar Lodi founded Agra in 1504 CE and shifted his capital there from Delhi. Before the Mughals made Agra famous (Taj Mahal, Red Fort of Agra), it was a Lodi capital. Sikandar Lodi is considered the greatest Lodi Sultan — efficient, culturally interested, but orthodox in religious policy.

questions.text: Timur's invasion of India in 1398 CE was directed against which dynasty's ruler?
questions.explanation: Timur (Tamerlane) invaded India in 1398 CE during the reign of Sultan Mahmud Shah Tughlaq (the last Tughlaq ruler with any real power). He sacked Delhi, massacred the population, and left with enormous plunder. The Tughlaq dynasty never recovered, leading to the rise of the Sayyid dynasty.

questions.text: Firuz Shah Tughlaq transported Ashokan pillars to Delhi from which locations?
questions.explanation: Firuz Shah Tughlaq transported two Ashokan pillars to Delhi: one from Topra (Haryana) and one from Meerut (UP). They were moved by river on specially constructed boats — a remarkable engineering feat. The Topra pillar now stands at Feroz Shah Kotla, Delhi.

questions.text: The 'Alai Darwaza' (1311 CE) near the Qutub Minar is significant because it is:
options: The tallest structure of the Khalji dynasty
The first building in India to use the true arch and true dome
The only building decorated with gold leaf
questions.explanation: The Alai Darwaza (gateway to the Qutub complex) built by Alauddin Khalji in 1311 CE is the first building in India to fully employ the true arch and true dome (as opposed to the earlier corbelled 'false arch'). This marks the beginning of authentic Islamic architectural technique in India.

questions.text: Malik Kafur was the general of which Delhi Sultan who conducted raids into South India?
questions.explanation: Malik Kafur was Alauddin Khalji's general (slave, bought for 1,000 dinars — hence 'Hazarddinari'). He led campaigns into the Deccan defeating Yadavas (Devagiri), Kakatiyas (Warangal), Hoysalas, and Pandyas — bringing enormous wealth north. After Alauddin's death, Kafur briefly controlled the government.

questions.text: Which department of the Delhi Sultanate handled military affairs?
questions.explanation: Diwan-i-Arz was the military department, headed by the Ariz-i-Mumalik. Diwan-i-Wizarat handled revenue and finance (headed by the Wazir). Diwan-i-Insha handled correspondence/state letters. Diwan-i-Risalat handled religious affairs and foreign correspondence.

questions.text: Balban's 'Theory of Kingship' stated that the king is:
options: First among equals among the nobles
Bound by the decisions of the Ulema
questions.explanation: Balban's theory of kingship held that the king is 'Zill-i-Ilahi' (Shadow of God on Earth) — an almost divine figure who must inspire awe. He introduced Persian court rituals: sijda (prostration) and paibos (kissing feet) to elevate the king's status. He ran a strict spy network and suppressed the Chahalgani nobles.

questions.text: The Delhi Sultanate ended when Ibrahim Lodi was defeated at the First Battle of Panipat by:
questions.explanation: Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat on April 21, 1526 CE — ending the Delhi Sultanate (which had lasted 320 years, 1206–1526) and establishing the Mughal Empire. Ibrahim Lodi died on the battlefield.
